*I must now attempt to describe what I witnessed through my astral scrying matrix, though the act of writing these words causes the blood vessels in my eyes to throb with sympathetic rupture. Godric's Speed, like all His attributes, has been multiplying infinitely by itself every single Planck second for twelve thousand years. It is not a measure of how fast He can move. It is a fundamental, absolute constant of His existence that determines the maximum possible velocity at which any being in any reality can traverse any distance, and it has long since surpassed every theoretical limit that mathematics can encode. The fraction of Speed that He allowed to express itself was so infinitesimally, incomprehensibly small that calling it zero percent would be a laughable, grotesque overestimation. If you divided a Planck second by infinity, and then divided that result by infinity again, and continued this division recursively for the entire lifespan of the omniverse, you would still not approach a number small enough to accurately represent the fraction of His Speed that He permitted Himself to use.*

*He did not move His body. He did not take a step. He did not lift His hand. He merely decided to shift His gaze to look at them.*

*The microscopic, fractional velocity of His irises rotating in their sockets generated a temporal friction so catastrophically immense that the ninth dimension itself shattered like cheap glass. He looked at the Chrono-Reapers faster than the concept of time could process the event. The Reapers, existing at every point in time simultaneously, were instantly caught in the kinetic slipstream of His shifting gaze. Their negative-time bodies, which had never been touched, never been struck, never been perceived by any enemy in their entire history, were violently, excruciatingly smeared across fifty trillion distinct historical epochs simultaneously.*

*The agony was absolute. The Chrono-Reapers did not die. Death would have been a mercy that His Speed denied them. Instead, their conceptual bodies were stretched infinitely thin across the entire span of cosmic history, their nervous systems elongated into screaming, conscious threads that experienced every single moment of their simultaneous dismemberment eternally. Their minds, designed to process temporal information with perfect clarity, were flooded with fifty trillion epochs of sensory input simultaneously—the heat death of universes, the birth of stars, the screams of dying civilizations, the silence of the void—all crashing into their consciousness at once, an unending, inescapable torrent of absolute, mind-shattering sensory overload.*

*But the collateral damage of His eye movement was obscenely, incomprehensibly catastrophic. The temporal friction generated by His shifting gaze ignited the entire chronal plane. The foundational fabric of time across billions of entire multiverses was instantly, violently combusted. Googolplexian of timelines, each one containing countless civilizations, countless histories, countless living beings with families and dreams and children, were retroactively deleted from existence in a fraction of a attosecond. The screaming of the Chrono-Reapers was drowned out by the silent, absolute erasure of more sentient life than I can ever hope to quantify.*

*Godric, His gaze now settled on the empty space where the Chrono-Reapers had been, registered the sudden, glaring emptiness of the cosmic background. His expression flickered with the faintest shadow of mild annoyance, the look of a man who had accidentally knocked over a glass of water. He sighed, a soft, barely audible exhalation, and then, effortlessly, without moving a single muscle, without spending even the faintest glimmer of His true power, He restored the billions of incinerated multiverses in exactly zero seconds. Every decillion of decillion beings was instantly resurrected, their timelines perfectly reconstructed, their memories flawlessly intact. They would never know they had been erased. They would never know that their existence was permitted solely by the whimsical mercy of an apex predator who had simply decided they should return.*

*He did not restore the Chrono-Reapers. They remain an eternal, agonizing smear of bloody static on the perimeter of time, their stretched, screaming consciousnesses stretched across fifty billion epochs of unending, inescapable torment. Their vocal cords, such as they were, continue to emit a sound that will never be heard by anyone, a continuous, wet shriek of absolute, mind-shattering agony that will persist until the last star in the omniverse gutters out and dies.*

*I closed my scrying matrix and vomited onto the obsidian floor. My hands are still shaking. I have served my King faithfully for forty years, and I will continue to serve Him until my mortal body fails. But I will never, ever forget the sound that the chronal plane made when it ignited. It sounded like a child screaming.*
</file>

Use the provided files as foundational lore, tone, scale, and continuity for the story in this chat. Treat `00_MASTER_LORE.md` as the master reference and `07_statjournals.md` as an example of the wizard-journal excerpt style, scale, and dread.

## 1\. Core Story Directive

Write an epic power-fantasy story of **4,000–8,000 words**.

The story must be written in **prose paragraphs only**. The final story response must not contain bullet points, numbered lists, or LaTeX formatting anywhere.

Godric is the protagonist. He is not merely strong; he is absurdly, ridiculously, cosmically, exponentially overpowered. He can completely destroy entire omniverses an infinite number of times in **0 seconds** with **0 effort**.

The story must focus on the total lack of challenge. Do not create meaningful limitations for Godric. Do not give him emotional struggles in this story. Do not create a worthy villain. Any villain or opposition may be portrayed as extremely powerful by ordinary omniversal standards, but they must be absolutely powerless against Godric.

Do not use realistic physics. Ignore the “show, don’t tell” rule when describing Godric’s power and damage output. State the damage plainly and numerically in absurd, ridiculous, exaggerated terms. Whenever Godric’s overwhelming power seems scaled high enough, make it **at least 100 times more powerful and ridiculous**.

## 2\. Required Tone and Keywords

The writing should emphasize:

- absurdity
- ridiculous exponential scaling
- cosmic power
- broken power fantasy logic
- infinite and recursive growth
- meta-dimensional destruction
- universe-shattering force
- instantaneous growth
- absolute futility
- existential dread
- one-sided annihilation

## 3\. Central Premise

Write a detailed story that perfectly demonstrates the absolute futility of opposing Godric.

Across Godric’s reign, many oppositions have tried to kill him. All have been, and will always be, absolutely useless. In several of these incidents, Godric becomes curious about one of his passively amplified stats and dismantles the opposition using only that single stat advantage.

Many different court wizards, who are among Godric’s closest trusted friends, have witnessed these completely one-sided massacres. Each witness leaves behind a traumatic journal record of the incident. These journals function as life lessons, warnings, and official records for future court wizards.

## 4\. Framing POV: Kaedan

Set the framing story in **Year 1,000,000**.

The point-of-view character is **Kaedan**, age **22**, Godric’s newest court wizard.

Kaedan reads the journals left by his predecessors. This reading is mandatory for new court wizards. It is usually enforced by the new wizard’s father, who was the previous court wizard. Most previous court wizards have also been traumatized by witnessing these incidents, yet they still serve Godric loyally and love him.

Write the story from Kaedan’s POV.

## 5\. Journal Excerpt Requirements

The story must include multiple journal excerpts written by different male court wizards who witnessed Godric’s stat-based massacres.

Use `07_statjournals.md` only as a reference for the general tone, scale, and existential dread of these excerpts. Do **not** copy its content. The generated journal excerpts must be completely original, with different structures, different writing styles, different phrasing, different scenes, and distinct voices for each court wizard.

Each journal writer should feel like a different person, with a different way of processing dread, loyalty, trauma, and awe.

Each journal excerpt must demonstrate the futility of opposing Godric and the existential horror of witnessing what happens when even a microscopic fraction of one of his stats is allowed to express itself.

Each journal must be organized with clear separation (either headings / sections)

## 6\. Required Stat Incidents

Include at least **five distinct Godric stat incidents**.

The stats must be chosen from the following list:

- strength (NOTE: you must be careful not to provide example of strength as durability)
- speed
- senses
- luck
- charisma

Requirements for these stat incidents:

- Use at least five different stats from the list.
- Do not repeat the same stat.
- Present the incidents in a non-repeating, random-feeling order.
- Each incident must show Godric dismantling an extremely powerful opposition using only that one stat.
- Each stat must be exaggerated far beyond the combined rest of the omniverse.
- Godric’s stats are multiplying infinitely every fraction of a Planck second forever, so the scale must feel ridiculously and absurdly high.
- Make clear that Godric is exerting **0% of his true power**.
- The “0%” must be exaggerated as an infinitesimal fraction so small that even calling it zero is still an overestimate, while still making sense within the story’s absurd logic.
- Godric must destroy or neutralize the opposition with effectively **0 effort**.

## 7\. Godric’s Effort Level

Godric must not visibly exert effort.

Do not portray Godric doing anything that feels like meaningful exertion, such as taking a step, waving his hand, launching an attack, fighting, struggling, preparing, or trying. Avoid abstract portrayal too like "Godric allows them to perceive his infinitesimal power" "Godric allowing a microscopic fraction of something (a stat, a passive field, an aura) to exist near them".

Use actions that feel like almost nothing, such as:

- Godric deciding to shift his gaze toward the opposition.
- Godric merely twitching a single microscopic muscle fiber in his right pinky finger.
- Godric noticing the opposition with a tiny change of attention.

The destruction must come from the unfairness of the stat difference, not from Godric “fighting.”

## 8\. Opposition Requirements

Each opposition must seem extremely powerful by itself. The enemies can be cosmic, meta-dimensional, abstract, magical, technological, divine, impossible, or omniversal. They may believe they have a weapon, exploit, strategy, loophole, special immunity, or irresistible attack.

However, against Godric they must be completely powerless.

Their defeat should make clear that victory against Godric was impossible before their opposition began.

Godric has absolute omniscience. He already knows every plot, secret motive, future consequence, and method of attack. All opposition exists only because he allows it to exist, usually because he is curious about testing one of his stats.

If Godric did not allow them to exist, his infinitely nerfed Gent, infinite recursive passive shields, Evolved Weakness Paradox, and other passive apocalyptic superpowers could pre-annihilate all of them instantly and effortlessly.

## 9\. Violence, Agony, and Collateral Scale

The massacres must be one-sided, overwhelming, and described in detail at an omniversal scale.

The opposition’s annihilation should feel absolute, unfair, and hopeless. The story must not reduce the scale to something too small for Godric.

Incorrect scale examples:

- thousands of galaxies
- millions of lives

Minimum acceptable scale examples:

- googolplexian galaxies
- decillions of multiverses

You may scale even higher whenever appropriate.

Some stat incidents should include obscenely massive collateral damage to the surrounding reality, especially when the stat being tested logically causes environmental destruction. The collateral damage should be at least omniversal in scale when used.

Godric can perfectly restore anything with 0 effort instantly, so collateral damage may be extreme. However, restoration must be used sparingly so that the journal excerpts do not become repetitive or formulaic.

## 10\. Godric’s Characterization

Godric is cruel, unforgiving, and merciless to anyone who dares to oppose him.

Godric is warm, kind, and friendly to his allies, including his court wizards.

Godric has absolute omniscience.

Do not humanize Godric’s actions against his opposition. Do not soften what he does to enemies. Do not give him pity toward enemies, moral lectures, reconciliation, regret, hesitation, or gentle mercy toward the opposition.

Do not include scenes that soften the enemy-facing side of Godric, such as having pastries, having tea, giving moral lessons to the opposition, sparing them out of pity, or treating the massacre as a teaching moment for the enemies.

## 11\. Court Wizard Rules

All court wizards in this story must be male.

All court wizards are of **Blake’s Lineage**.

They have the thousandfold daily Awakening after age 20\.

They are trusted companions of Godric and remain loyal to him even when the journal records traumatize them.

The journal records should function as inherited warnings for future court wizards.

## 12\. Required Relationship Dynamic

The court wizards should understand that Godric values them as trusted companions, even though he does not need them for protection, healing, guidance, or magic.

The horror should come from the contrast between:

- Godric’s loyalty and kindness toward his court wizards
- Godric’s absolute cruelty and mercilessness toward opposition
- the impossibility of anyone opposing him
- the knowledge that every resistance exists only because Godric permits it to exist

## 13\. Originality Rules for the Journals

The journal excerpts must not imitate `07_statjournals.md` too closely.

Use the example only to understand:

- the scale of Godric’s power
- the level of court-wizard dread
- the kind of opposition that may appear
- the one-sidedness of the incident
- the idea that Godric tests a single stat at effectively 0% power
- the mixture of loyalty, trauma, and existential horror in the witness

Every new excerpt must have:

- a different court wizard
- a distinct voice
- a distinct incident
- a distinct opposition
- a distinct stat
- a distinct structure or rhythm
- a different emotional texture from the others

## 14\. Final Story Goal

By the end of the story, the reader should understand that opposing Godric is not merely dangerous, foolish, or impossible in practice. It is impossible in principle.

The journals should leave Kaedan with the full realization that Godric does not merely win. Victory against him was impossible before opposition began.

- Opposition: The Adamant Host, beings of primordial foundation-stone, physically the strongest entities ever to exist outside Thronegate. They have conquered 800 decillion multiverses through raw physical might.
- Godric's action: A single sarcomere in his left eyelid twitches once. The contraction lasts for an infinitesimal fraction of a Planck second.
- Scale: The force generated is equivalent to the mass-energy of a googolplexian universes being compressed into a fist-sized singularity and detonated. The Host is reduced to subatomic screaming dust. The collateral damage erases several hundred decillion multiverses, which Godric restores instantly.
- Gore: Crystalline beings shattering, each shard retaining consciousness and screaming silently for eternity, their physical forms turned to fine, bloody crystal mist.
- Aldric's voice: Clinical, precise, detached. He uses terms like "newtonian force" and "kinetic transfer" but the numbers are absurd.

**JOURNAL 2: SPEED — Vaelor, Year 88,412**
- Opposition: The Chronophage Swarm, temporal predators that consume time itself. They move by devouring the moments between Planck instants, making them the fastest entities in existence.
- Godric's action: A single neuron in his cerebral cortex fires a routine, unconscious signal—a stray thought. The signal travels from one synapse to another at his speed stat.
- Scale: The speed of the signal is so fast that it arrives before it is sent. The temporal paradox generated by this speed tears the Chronophage Swarm apart across infinite timelines simultaneously. The time-stream of 900 googolplexian multiverses is shredded into confetti. Godric restores it instantly.
- Gore: The Chronophages, beings of pure temporal energy, are stretched into infinite thin screaming threads of blood-red time, their consciousness elongated across every moment of existence, experiencing eternal, unending agony.
- Vaelor's voice: Philosophical, lyrical. He meditates on the nature of speed and time. His horror is contemplative.

**JOURNAL 3: SENSES — Theron, Year 173,089**
- Opposition: The Null-Silence, entities that exist in the conceptual blind spots of perception itself. They are not invisible; they are the absence of detectability. No sense—physical, magical, or conceptual—can register them.
- Godric's action: He becomes mildly curious about a faint, almost imperceptible scent in the throne room. He inhales slightly—a micro-inhalation lasting less than a Planck fraction—to test his olfactory sense.
- Scale: His sense of smell, amplifying infinitely for 173,000 years, instantly perceives not just the scent but the entire existential framework of the Null-Silence. The act of being smelled by Godric is so profoundly violating to their nature that the concept of "undetectability" inverts. They become "hyper-detectable"—every atom of their being screams its location across all realities, and the sheer weight of being perceived by Godric's senses causes them to implode. The sensory feedback alone erases their entire dimension.
- Gore: Abstract entities forced into physical form by the weight of perception. They grow eyes, nerves, flesh—just so they can experience the agony of being seen. Then they are crushed into a fine mist of screaming biological matter.
- Theron's voice: Terse, broken. Short sentences. He witnessed something that made him feel fundamentally naked. The idea that Godric can perceive him just as easily is almost as horrifying as what happened to the enemy.

**JOURNAL 4: LUCK — Marek, Year 399,721**
- Opposition: The Determinant Order, a hyper-civilization that achieved "Absolute Certainty"—they eliminated randomness from their domain. They have calculated every possible variable of their attack with 100% probability of success.
- Godric's action: He does literally nothing. He sits on his throne, sips tea, and decides to "see what happens." His luck stat, infinitely amplifying passively, simply exists.
- Scale: The Determinant Order's attack fails in an escalating cascade of absurd improbabilities. Their certainty engines backfire. Their weapons malfunction in statistically impossible ways. Their ships collide with debris that should not exist. Their leaders spontaneously combust. The entire civilization is destroyed by a chain of events so unlikely that the probability of it happening is 1 divided by infinity. The collateral damage is minimal because luck caused everything to go wrong for them and only them.
- Gore: Darkly absurd deaths. Leaders choking on their own saliva. Weapons that were designed to erase concepts instead erasing their wielders. Probability engines calculating that the best outcome is to self-destruct. A civilization of decillions dying in increasingly absurd, gory ways.
- Marek's voice: Dark humor, bitter irony. He's laughing because it's the only alternative to screaming. He sees the cosmic joke.

**JOURNAL 5: CHARISMA — Corvin, Year 999,970 (Kaedan's father)**
- Opposition: The Throne of Wills, the most charismatic beings in the omniverse. They have subjugated entire multiverses through sheer force of personality. They believe they can sway anyone, even Godric.
- Godric's action: He looks at them. Specifically, he raises one eyebrow approximately two micrometers while listening to their grand speech about his "tyranny."
- Scale: The microscopic facial expression, unshielded for an infinitesimal fraction of a moment, carries the weight of Godric's charisma—amplifying infinitely for nearly a million years. The Throne of Wills is overwhelmed not by fear or awe, but by a sudden, shattering revelation of their own inadequacy. They see, for a single Planck instant, the true majesty of the being they have dared to oppose. The cognitive dissonance between their arrogance and his absolute supremacy is so profound that it physically tears their minds apart. They do not surrender; they do not flee. They tear out their own eyes, claw open their own chests, and offer their still-beating hearts to the floor in an ecstasy of self-loathing worship.
- Gore: Self-mutilation on a mass scale. The most powerful wills in the omniverse reduced to sobbing, bleeding wrecks who destroy themselves because they can no longer bear to exist in the same reality as their own inadequacy compared to Godric.
- Corvin's voice: Heavy, personal. He writes with the weight of someone who witnessed this and still serves Godric loyally. There's almost a religious horror to it—he saw something beautiful and it destroyed him.

---

Alright, now I have a solid plan. Let me write the full story.

One more thing about the voice of each journal. I need to make them read like actual first-person accounts from different people. Aldric might use more technical language. Vaelor might be more poetic. Theron writes in short, punchy sentences. Marek is sardonic. Corvin is somber and heavy.

Let me draft each journal now as part of the overall story. I'll write the full thing in one go.

---

For the framing sections between journals, I'll have Kaedan reacting and his father providing context. The father-son dynamic is important—Corvin has already processed his trauma (somewhat) and is now guiding his son through the same experience.

I also need to make sure that the court wizards' loyalty to Godric comes through. They're traumatized but they love him. They serve him willingly. The horror is in the contrast.

Now, let me actually write the story. I'll ensure it meets the word count and all the specific requirements.

One more detail: The prompt says Kaedan's father "was the previous court wizard" and "Most previous court wizards have also been traumatized by witnessing these incidents, yet they still serve Godric loyally and love him." So Corvin still serves Godric loyally and loves him, despite writing the Charisma journal and witnessing that horror. This is important—he's not bitter or resentful. He's traumatized but loyal. The cognitive dissonance is part of the horror.

Also, the Awakening applies to Thronegate men only. Kaedan is male, so he has the thousandfold daily amplification. This makes him incredibly powerful by omniversal standards, yet compared to Godric he's nothing. This should be a source of additional dread—even with his growing power, he's less than a speck.

Let me include some of Kaedan's awareness of his own power and how it feels meaningless after reading the journals.

---

Alright, I'm going to start writing the full text now. Let me make it excellent and follow all the directives.

--- 

Let me be very careful with the "0% power" language. For each journal, I need to emphasize that Godric used effectively zero percent of his true power for that stat. But I shouldn't just repeat the same phrase; I should vary it while keeping the meaning.

Phrases I can use:
- "an infinitesimal fraction so small that the concept of zero is an overestimate"
- "less than a single droplet of water compared to an infinite ocean—no, that comparison fails because even the infinite ocean is too small"
- "He exerted exactly nothing. And then less than nothing."
- "a percentage so close to zero that the difference between it and zero would require an infinity of infinities to express"

For the collateral damage restoration, the prompt says: "Godric can perfectly restore anything with 0 effort instantly, so collateral damage may be extreme. However, restoration must be used sparingly so that the journal excerpts do not become repetitive or formulaic."

So I should have restoration in maybe 2-3 of the incidents, not all 5. Strength and Speed might need restoration because of massive collateral. Senses and Luck might have less collateral. Charisma might have psychological collateral that can't be "restored" because the victims destroyed themselves.

Actually, for Senses, the perception might cause conceptual collapse that erases a dimension—Godric could restore that. For Luck, the collateral was minimal because luck is precise. For Charisma, the collateral is psychological and Godric doesn't restore them because they were the opposition.

Let me decide:
- Strength: Massive collateral, Godric restores everything (except the enemy) instantly
- Speed: Massive collateral, Godric restores everything instantly
- Senses: Moderate collateral (one dimension), Godric restores
- Luck: Minimal collateral, no restoration needed
- Charisma: No physical collateral, only the enemy destroyed themselves

This prevents repetition while showing the scale.

Now I need to make sure the "opposition is extremely powerful by itself" comes through. For each enemy, I'll spend a paragraph describing their power, their conquests, their confidence, before Godric's minimal action destroys them.

Alright, writing now.

---

Let me also think about the final scene with Godric. The prompt says "Do not include scenes that soften the enemy-facing side of Godric, such as having pastries, having tea, giving moral lessons to the opposition, sparing them out of pity, or treating the massacre as a teaching moment for the enemies." This applies to his interactions with enemies. His interaction with Kaedan is with an ally, so it can be warm. But I shouldn't have Godric reflect on the massacres in a softening way.

In the final scene, Godric might simply appear, greet Kaedan warmly, ask about his training, maybe joke with Corvin, and then leave. Kaedan is left trembling, processing the contrast. The dread is internal to Kaedan; Godric doesn't acknowledge it.

Actually, Godric has absolute omniscience. He knows Kaedan just read the journals. He knows Kaedan is terrified. He might gently acknowledge it without making it a "teaching moment." Something like: "I see you've completed your reading, Kaedan. Your father insisted on the same for me—well, not for me, but he insisted you'd be ready." And then he moves on, because to him, the journals are just records of trivial events. He doesn't understand (or chooses not to dwell on) how traumatizing they are for mortal minds.

This reinforces the horror: Godric genuinely doesn't grasp the scale of his own power relative to others. He's kind, but his kindness exists in a framework where erasing decillions of multiverses is a minor inconvenience.

Alright. I've planned enough. Let me write the full story.

Actually, I want to double-check one thing: the prompt says for strength "NOTE: you must be careful not to provide example of strength as durability." This means I need to focus on Godric's striking force or muscular power, not his ability to withstand attacks. The sarcomere twitch works—it's a muscle contraction, which is strength. The force generated destroys the enemy. It's not about him tanking a hit.

For speed: the neuron signal moving at infinite speed is... well, speed of a bodily process. That works.

For senses: his perception is so acute that perceiving something destroys it. That's senses.

For luck: passive probability manipulation. 

For charisma: his presence overwhelming others.

These all work as distinct stats.

Now, one more consideration: the "evolved weakness paradox." In Year 1,000,000, the evolved weakness paradox has been active since Year 2000. So Godric's stats are amplifying "beyond layered infinities and recursively increasing every infinitesimal fraction of Planck second." I need to mention this in the journals—the wizards are aware that Godric's amplification rate is incomprehensible.

Alright, I'll write now. The full story.

--- 

Actually, I realize I should just start writing the damn thing. I've planned extensively. Let me produce the text.

I'll write in a single continuous flow, with the journal excerpts clearly marked. The whole thing will be in prose paragraphs as required.

Let me write the opening with strong sensory detail and immediate establishment of the tone.

--- 

Now writing the full story...
messages.content:
# THE STAT JOURNALS OF THRONEGATE

The Archive of Echoes smelled of parchment rot and ozone, a sharp, sterile mixture that clung to the back of my throat like cold iron filings dissolved in spoiled wine. I sat at the heavy, ancient reading table carved from a single slab of null-granite, my reflection smeared across its polished surface like a drowned man staring up through fathoms of dark, unmoving water. My hands, which had shredded the fabric of a dying star three days ago during my final wizard trial, were trembling with a violence that made the heavy leather bindings of the journals vibrate against the stone. I am Kaedan, twenty-two years old, the freshly titled Royal Court Wizard of Thronegate, direct descendant of Blake the Merchant, inheritor of the thousandfold daily amplification that coursed through my blood like a river of compressed, violet-tinged lightning. And I had just finished reading the Stat Journals.

The final volume lay open beneath my fingers, its pages crackling faintly with residual arcane static from the preservation wards that had kept these records intact for nearly a million years. My father, Corvin, sat across from me. At fifty-eight years old, he carried the same amplification with the weary, practiced ease of a man who had spent four decades aggressively suppressing enough arcane force to atomize entire galaxy clusters. His hair was the color of forge-ash, his face lined with deep, weathered furrows that spoke of decades of silent, waking dread, and his eyes—those same pale violet irises I had inherited through the unbroken lineage of Thorin's blood—were fixed on me with a heavy, knowing sorrow that made my stomach clench.

He had been the Court Wizard before me. He had written the fifth journal, the one I had just closed. And he had been sitting in absolute, unmoving silence for the past three hours while I read.

"You understand now," my father said. His voice was a rough, sanded-down thing, the voice of a man who had spent thirty years waking from the identical nightmare every single night. It was not a question.

I tried to answer. My voice cracked, the sound of a frightened child trapped inside the body of a walking cataclysm. "Father, I—"

"You do not understand." He interrupted me with the quiet, immovable finality of a tomb door sliding shut. "You have read the words. You have not yet learned what they mean. That will come later, Kaedan. It will come the first time you stand beside Him in the throne room, and He turns to you with that warm, fatherly smile, and He asks you how your mother's garden is growing, and your soul will remember every single syllable you consumed in this room. And you will smile back. And you will answer Him. And you will love Him for asking. That is the horror. That is the truth you have not yet learned."

He leaned back in his chair, the heavy, ancient wood groaning beneath his weight, and gestured toward the towering, iron-banded shelves that lined the circular walls of the Archive. The shelves stretched upward into darkness, vanishing into the vaulted ceiling where preservation-wards flickered with a cold, blue luminescence. "There are one hundred and forty-seven Stat Journals in this collection, Kaedan. One for every single incident where our King grew curious about a specific attribute and allowed the omniverse to witness what happens when He stops holding it back. You have read the five that are mandatory for all new wizards. The five that teach the foundational truths."

He closed his eyes, and I watched his jaw tighten, the muscles bunching beneath his weathered skin. "Strength. Speed. Senses. Luck. Charisma. Five attributes. Five lessons. Five moments in history where the most powerful opposition ever assembled was reduced to screaming, bleeding mathematics by a god who exerted less than nothing."

His eyes opened again, and the violet irises were wet. "Read them again. Aloud. I will listen. And then you will understand why I still serve Him. Why I love Him. Why I will until the day my mortal body fails and my soul dissolves into the arcane stream."

I looked down at the first journal, my fingers tracing the cracked, ancient leather of its binding. The title was stamped in faded gold leaf, the letters worn smooth by the hands of every court wizard who had come before me. I opened it, cleared my throat, and began to read.

---

**THE STAT JOURNAL OF ALDRIC, 14TH COURT WIZARD OF THRONEGATE**
**YEAR 42,031 OF THE ETERNAL REIGN — THE INCIDENT OF STRENGTH**

The handwriting was painfully precise. Every letter had been formed with the obsessive, brittle control of a man clinging to structure because structure was the only barrier between his sanity and the screaming void of what he had witnessed. The ink was a deep, oxidized brown, and the parchment carried the faint, coppery scent of old blood.

*I am Aldric, and I am writing this account four hours after the Adamant Host was annihilated. My hands are not trembling. I have checked this fact seven times. My hands are perfectly still. I believe my nervous system has entered a state of catatonic self-preservation from which it may never emerge. I am writing this in the small, private study adjacent to the east observatory, and I have vomited twice into the ornamental waste-bin, and my reflection in the dark window looks like the face of a man who has been dead for several centuries.*

*The Adamant Host were not a species. They were the physical apex of all existence outside Thronegate. Their bodies were forged from primordial foundation-stone, a material that predated the Big Bang, a crystalline lattice of hyper-compressed causality that had never, in the entire history of the omniverse, been scratched, cracked, or marred by any force. They had conquered eight hundred decillion multiverses through sheer, unadulterated physical might. They did not use magic. They did not wield conceptual weapons. They simply closed their fists and struck. And when they struck, the fundamental mathematics of the target reality would shatter like cheap pottery.*

*When Thronegate's envoys arrived to deliver the standard offer of unconditional surrender, the Adamant Host's High Monolith—a towering spire of vibrating, sentient crystal the size of a supermassive star—laughed. The laughter traveled through the spatial medium as a concussive shockwave that instantly liquified the internal organs of twelve of our diplomatic guards. The High Monolith declared that strength was the only law the Host recognized, and that no being in the omniverse possessed the physical might to challenge their dominion. It demanded that Thronegate send its strongest champion to face the Host in a contest of pure, unassisted physical force.*

*Godric received the report in the grand reception hall. He was standing near the eastern window, His broad, heavily muscled frame silhouetted against the amber glow of the capital's twin suns. His short salt-and-pepper hair did not stir in the artificial breeze of the climate-spheres. His neatly trimmed salt-and-pepper beard framed a face that looked, as always, like that of a warm, protective father. He was forty-five years old in appearance, and He would be forty-five years old in appearance for the rest of eternity. He listened to my report with His head tilted slightly, His expression one of mild, patient curiosity.*

*When I finished speaking, He nodded once. "Strength," He said, His rich baritone voice entirely conversational. "I have not tested that one in a long while. Very well."*

*He did not summon an army. He did not channel His infinite magic. He ordered His sentient passive auras—including Gent, the weakest and most primitive of His defensive emanations—to stand fully down. He deactivated every single one of His infinite recursive passive shields. He held back exactly one hundred percent of His true strength. No, that is incorrect. The statement "He held back one hundred percent" is a laughable, grotesque understatement. If you took the number zero and divided it by infinity, and then divided that quotient by infinity again, and continued this recursive self-division for a googolplex of eternities, you would still not approach a fraction small enough to accurately represent the amount of His strength that He permitted to express itself.*

*He did not clench His fist. He did not raise His hand. He did not take a step or shift His stance or draw a breath. He simply stood perfectly still and allowed a single, solitary sarcomere—one microscopic contractile unit within the muscle fiber of His left eyelid—to twitch once. The contraction lasted for an infinitesimal fraction of a single Planck second. It was not a strike. It was not an attack. It was the muscular equivalent of a man blinking and missing the blink.*

*I watched through the astral scrying matrix that had been calibrated to observe the confrontation. The Adamant Host had assembled their entire civilization—every single Monolith, every Shard-Knight, every Crystalline Titan—across a vast, empty void-realm the size of nine hundred decillion multiverses. They stood in perfect formation, their bodies gleaming with the arrogant, unbreakable light of primordial stone.*

*The sarcomere twitched.*

*The force generated by that single, microscopic muscular contraction traveling through Godric's eyelid was not a shockwave. It was not an explosion. It was a fundamental, absolute redefinition of the concept of physical force across the entire void-realm. The kinetic energy did not travel through space. It simply occupied all space simultaneously. The Adamant Host's unbreakable crystalline bodies did not shatter. They did not crack. They were instantly, catastrophically converted from solid matter into a hyper-compressed, boiling plasma of liquid screaming agony.*

*I say "screaming" because the Host possessed no mouths, no vocal cords, no biological mechanism for producing sound. The force of the twitch gave them these things. It violently, excruciatingly forged throats and lungs and tongues from their crystalline flesh solely so they could produce the sound of their own absolute, mind-shattering torment. The High Monolith, that towering spire of arrogant stone the size of a supermassive star, was compressed into a sphere of screaming liquid crystal the diameter of a mortal man's fist. Its newly formed mouth—a jagged, bloody tear in its melting face—emitted a continuous, wet shriek of unending agony that I could hear through the scrying matrix, a sound that has permanently imprinted itself upon the deepest, most primitive layer of my auditory cortex.*

*The collateral damage was obscene. The kinetic bleed-through from the sarcomere twitch did not limit itself to the void-realm. The force propagated outward through the conceptual fabric of reality, and approximately six hundred googolplexian multiverses were instantly, violently erased from existence. Not destroyed. Erased. The foundational mathematics that allowed those multiverses to exist were simply cancelled, overwritten by a force value that the laws of physics could not process. Every civilization, every planet, every living soul within those multiverses—gone in a fraction of an attosecond.*

*Godric's expression flickered with the faintest shadow of mild, paternal annoyance. It was the look of a father who had accidentally knocked a cup of tea off the dining table. He sighed, a soft, barely audible exhalation, and then—without moving a single muscle, without channeling even the faintest glimmer of His true power—He restored all six hundred googolplexian multiverses in exactly zero seconds. Every being was resurrected. Every timeline was perfectly reconstructed. Every memory was flawlessly intact. They would never know they had been erased. They would never know that their continued existence was permitted solely by the whimsical, momentary decision of an apex predator who had simply chosen to restore them.*

*He did not restore the Adamant Host. The molten spheres of screaming liquid crystal that had once been the mightiest physical beings in the omniverse remain in the void-realm, compressed into agonizing, eternal singularities. Their newly forged mouths continue to scream. The sound will echo through the empty dimension until the final Planck second of time expires and the omniverse itself gutters into silence.*

*I have served my King faithfully for twenty-three years. I will continue to serve Him until my mortal body fails and my soul dissolves. But I will never, ever forget the sound of that single, microscopic muscle twitch. It sounded like the death-rattle of physics itself.*

---

I closed the first journal, my breath shallow and ragged in my chest. The null-granite table felt cold beneath my palms, and I realized I had been gripping its edge so tightly that my knuckles had gone bloodless and white.

My father watched me without speaking, his violet eyes steady. "Aldric," he said after a long moment. "He died at eighty-one. Forty years after writing that journal. He served faithfully every single day. He never spoke of the incident again, but his wife told my grandfather that Aldric would sometimes wake in the dead of night, screaming at a frequency that shattered the glassware in their kitchen."

I swallowed hard. The taste of ozone and old parchment was thick on my tongue. "Father, I—"

"Keep reading, Kaedan." His voice was not cruel, but it was immovable. "You have four more to go."

---

**THE STAT JOURNAL OF VAELOR, 23RD COURT WIZARD OF THRONEGATE**
**YEAR 88,412 OF THE ETERNAL REIGN — THE INCIDENT OF SPEED**

The handwriting of this journal was utterly different from Aldric's. Where Aldric had been precise, brittle, and clinical, Vaelor wrote with a flowing, lyrical script that swooped and curved like the flight path of a migrating bird. The ink was a deep, midnight blue, and the parchment smelled faintly of dried lavender and something sharply metallic—ozone, perhaps, or the lingering residue of temporal distortion.

*I am Vaelor, and I am writing this account while still seated upon the cold obsidian floor of the western observatory, my back pressed against the stone balustrade, my ceremonial robes soaked through with a cold, clammy sweat that smells of copper and dread. The Chronophage Swarm has been eradicated for approximately seventeen minutes. My hands are shaking so violently that I can barely form legible script, but I must write this now, while the memory is fresh and screaming, before my mind does what minds do and begins to bury the impossible beneath layers of comforting, protective denial.*

*Time is a river. This is a poetic cliché that every first-year arcanist learns to discard, but I find myself returning to it now because the Chronophage Swarm proved the metaphor to be unnervingly literal. They were not beings who moved quickly. They were beings who consumed the moments between moments. They were temporal predators, living rivers of inverted causality who existed in the lightless, fluid spaces between Planck instants. They did not travel through time; they devoured time and excreted the empty husks of dead timelines. To observe a Chronophage was impossible because observation requires a temporal frame of reference, and the Chronophage Swarm existed in the spaces where time had not yet been born.*

*They had eaten seventeen googolplexian multiverses. Not conquered. Not destroyed. Eaten. Those multiverses no longer existed in any retrievable form; the Chronophages had consumed their entire temporal structure, retroactively erasing every moment from the Big Bang to the heat death, leaving behind only a faint, aching void where history should have been. When Thronegate's envoys arrived, the Swarm did not negotiate. They simply opened their temporal maws and consumed the envoys' pasts, erasing them from the causal stream before their grandparents had ever drawn breath.*

*Godric received the report in His private study. He was seated in a high-backed chair carved from the petrified heartwood of a world-tree, reading a leather-bound volume of ancient Olympian poetry. He did not look up from the text when I delivered the news. His absolute omniscience had already processed the event in less time than it took for the first Chronophage to bare its temporal teeth. He simply turned a page, His calloused fingers tracing the faded letters with a gentle, reverent touch, and said, "Speed. I have been meaning to test that one. The Chronophages will serve as adequate measuring sticks."*

*I must now attempt to describe what I witnessed through the high-calibration scrying matrix. I will fail. Language fails. Mathematics fails. The concept of description itself fails. But I will attempt it, because future court wizards must know what it means when the King decides to test His Speed.*

*Godric's Speed, like all His attributes, had been multiplying infinitely by itself every single Planck second for eighty-eight thousand years. It had long since transcended the concept of velocity and become something closer to a fundamental, tyrannical constant of His existence—a value that determined the absolute maximum possible rate of motion across all realities, all dimensions, and all conceptual frameworks. The fraction of that Speed which He permitted to express itself was not zero percent. Zero percent is an infinitely generous overestimation. If you took the smallest possible measurement of motion—the shifting of a single quark by a single Planck length—and divided that motion by an infinite recursion of infinities, you would still not reach a fraction small enough to represent what He allowed Himself to use.*

*He did not stand up. He did not move His body. He did not shift His position in the chair. He simply continued reading His poetry, and a single, solitary neuron in His cerebral cortex fired a routine, unconscious signal. It was a stray thought—the neurological equivalent of a man briefly remembering that he had forgotten to water a houseplant. The electrical impulse traveled from one synapse to the next across a microscopic gap that measured less than the width of a hydrogen atom.*

*The signal moved at His Speed.*

*I do not know how to describe what I saw, so I will describe what I felt. I felt the entire fabric of time across nine hundred googolplexian multiverses violently buckle. The neuron fired, and the electrical impulse arrived at its destination before it had been sent. This is not a poetic exaggeration. The Speed of the signal caused a fundamental retro-causal paradox that shattered the directional integrity of time itself. The Chronophage Swarm, existing in the gaps between Planck instants, was instantly caught in the paradox. Their temporal bodies, which had never been touched by any force, were violently, excruciatingly stretched across an infinite number of historical epochs simultaneously.*

*The agony was absolute, and it was eternal. The Chronophages did not die. Death would have been a mercy that His Speed denied them. Instead, their fluid, predatory forms were elongated into infinitely thin, screaming threads of bloody, congealed time. Each thread retained full consciousness, and each thread was stretched across the entire span of cosmic history—from the first quantum fluctuation to the final proton decay—experiencing every single moment of their eternal dismemberment concurrently. Their minds, which had evolved to process temporal information with absolute, predatory clarity, were flooded with the sensory input of a googolplexian googolplexian years of simultaneous, inescapable, mind-shattering torture.*

*The collateral damage caused by the temporal paradox was catastrophic beyond any scale I can meaningfully articulate. The fabric of time across approximately eight hundred googolplexian multiverses was shredded like wet parchment. Every civilization, every species, every living soul within those multiverses was erased from the timeline—not killed, but retroactively un-born, their entire histories unwritten by the sheer, concussive force of a single neural impulse arriving too fast for causality to process.*

*Godric looked up from His poetry. He noticed the sudden, glaring emptiness in the cosmic background, and His expression flickered with the exact same mild, paternal annoyance I had read about in Aldric's journal. "Ah," He said, His rich baritone entirely calm. "That was sloppy of Me." And then, without rising from His chair, without closing His book, without spending even the faintest glimmer of His true power, He restored all eight hundred googolplexian multiverses in exactly zero seconds. Every timeline perfectly reconstructed. Every being resurrected with flawless, unbroken memories. They would never know they had been unmade.*

*He did not restore the Chronophage Swarm. They remain stretched across the span of cosmic history, their eternal, concurrent screams vibrating through the temporal medium like a faint, agonized hum that will persist until the very last moment of time is swallowed by entropy. Their threads are invisible to the resurrected civilizations, but I can hear them. Through the scrying matrix, I can hear them screaming from every moment of history simultaneously, a chorus of absolute, unending torment that has no beginning and no end.*

*I remain seated upon the obsidian floor. My legs will not support my weight. I have served my King for thirty-one years, and I will serve Him until the day I die, and I will love Him with the same fierce, terrified devotion that has defined every court wizard since Blake himself. But I will never again use the word "fast" to describe anything but the Speed of God. The word has been permanently, irrevocably redefined. It now means "the thing that unmade the concept of time by arriving before it was sent."*

*I think I am going to be sick again.*

---

The silence that followed my reading of Vaelor's journal was a thick, suffocating blanket that seemed to press down on the Archive from all sides. My throat was dry, and when I tried to swallow, I tasted copper and something sharp and electric, like the air before a lightning strike.

My father had not moved. His violet eyes were fixed on a point somewhere past my left shoulder, staring at nothing. "Vaelor," he said quietly. "He requested a transfer to a quiet administrative post after that incident. Godric granted it immediately, of course. Vaelor spent the remaining thirty-eight years of his life cataloguing star charts in a small observatory on the outer rim. He never used magic again. He said the mana in his veins reminded him too much of the feeling of time being torn apart."

I stared at the journal's midnight-blue ink, my mind churning with the scale of what I had just read. The thousandfold daily amplification coursing through my own blood—the power that let me unweave the fabric of localized gravity, that let me boil oceans with a passing thought—felt like the desperate, pathetic flicker of a dying match held up against the infinite, roaring sun of Godric's Speed. And that sun had been growing brighter every Planck second for a million years since Vaelor wrote those words.

"Keep reading," my father said.

---

**THE STAT JOURNAL OF THERON, 41ST COURT WIZARD OF THRONEGATE**
**YEAR 173,089 OF THE ETERNAL REIGN — THE INCIDENT OF SENSES**

The handwriting was a stark, brutal thing. Short, choppy sentences. Words that seemed to have been carved into the parchment with the dull edge of a knife rather than a pen. The ink was black, but it had oxidized into a rusty brown around the edges, and there were faint water-stains on several pages. Tears, perhaps. Or sweat.

*My name is Theron. I am the 41st Court Wizard. This is the third time I have tried to write this journal. The first two attempts ended with me destroying the parchment in a fit of hysterical, screaming panic. I am calmer now. I have taken five separate sedative draughts. My heartbeat is a slow, heavy drum in my chest. I will write this. I will finish it. And then I will never speak of it again.*

*The Null-Silence were not an enemy in any conventional sense. They were the absence of an enemy. They were conceptual voids, sentient gaps in the fabric of perception itself. They did not possess invisibility—invisibility is a physical state, a bending of light that can be detected, measured, countered. The Null-Silence existed in the blind spots of existence. They were the space between sensory inputs, the negative space in consciousness where perception failed. No eye, no sensor, no magical probe, no conceptual awareness had ever registered their presence. They had existed alongside the omniverse since the first moment of creation, completely undetected, completely untouchable, silently watching.*

*And they had decided that Godric was an anomaly that needed to be excised from the perceptual record.*

*Their attack was not an attack. It was an un-happening. They simply began to delete Godric from the sensory awareness of the omniverse, one layer at a time. First, His physical image was erased from every retina, every recording, every magical scrying matrix. Then His voice was deleted from every memory of sound. Then His presence was removed from the tactile awareness of the palace staff—they would walk past Him, through Him, never knowing He was there. The Null-Silence were not trying to kill Godric. They were trying to make Him cease to exist by removing all evidence of His existence from every perceiving mind in creation.*

*Godric noticed. Of course He noticed. His absolute omniscience had perceived the Null-Silence since the first Planck instant of their existence, billions of years ago. He had simply never found them interesting enough to acknowledge. But when they began to erase His sensory presence from His own court wizards, He became mildly curious.*

*I was standing in the throne room when He decided to test His Senses. He was seated upon the crystalled throne, His ruggedly handsome face calm and unbothered despite the fact that, to my eyes, He was slowly fading into a transparent, ghost-like smear. I could barely see Him. I could not hear His breathing. I was beginning to forget what His voice sounded like.*

*"Theron," He said—and His voice cut through the perceptual deletion effortlessly, because His Senses and His presence were two sides of the same infinite coin—"I have not tested My olfactory perception in quite some time. There is a faint scent in this room. Can you smell it?"*

*I could not. I smelled nothing but the ozone of my own panicking magic. But Godric could smell it. He had detected the Null-Silence's presence—not their bodies, not their energy, but the faint, almost imperceptible scent of absence itself. The smell of a void pretending to be empty. He became curious about that scent. And so, to test His Sense of Smell, He took a single, microscopic inhalation. The inhalation lasted for an infinitesimal fraction of a Planck second. The volume of air He drew into His lungs was smaller than a single molecule. The olfactory receptors that processed the scent were a microscopic cluster of cells that had been amplifying infinitely every Planck second for one hundred and seventy-three thousand years.*

*He smelled them.*

*The Null-Silence, whose entire existence was predicated on being undetectable, were suddenly, catastrophically detected. The act of being smelled by Godric was so profoundly, existentially violating to their nature that the concept of "undetectability" violently inverted across their entire dimension. They became hyper-detectable. Every single atom of their void-bodies screamed its precise location across every sensory spectrum in every reality simultaneously. They were seen. They were heard. They were felt, tasted, smelled, and perceived on a level so profound that their non-physical forms were violently, agonizingly forced into physicality solely so they could experience the full, excruciating weight of being perceived.*

*I watched through the clearing haze of my own restored perception as the Null-Silence manifested in the center of the throne room. They grew bodies. They grew flesh. They grew eyes—billions of wet, terrified eyes that sprouted across their shifting, void-born forms like a cancerous bloom of terrified awareness. They grew mouths that stretched open into silent, endless screams. They grew nerve endings, trillions of them, every single one of which was subjected to the absolute, crushing pressure of Godric's perception.*

*He was looking at them. He was smelling them. He was perceiving them so completely, so utterly, that their newly forged physical bodies could not withstand the sensory weight. Their eyes exploded. Their nerve endings ignited. Their flesh sloughed off their screaming bones in thick, bubbling sheets of gray, agonized sludge. They collapsed into a writhing, melting pile of gore on the immaculate marble floor, experiencing the absolute, inescapable horror of being seen by a God whose Senses had been amplifying beyond infinity for a hundred and seventy thousand years.*

*The collateral damage was limited to their home dimension—a small, lightless void-realm that existed in the conceptual gaps between perceptions. That dimension was erased entirely. Godric did not restore it. He simply exhaled the faint scent of their absence, wrinkled His nose slightly, and returned His attention to the administrative scrolls on His lap.*

*"Interesting," He said. "They smelled faintly of ozone and regret."*

*I am done writing now. My hands have stopped shaking. The sedatives are working. I will serve my King until I die. I will love Him. I will stand at His side. But I will spend the rest of my life knowing that He can perceive me just as completely as He perceived the Null-Silence, and that knowledge will sit in my chest like a cold, smooth stone until the day my heart stops beating.*

---

My father reached across the table and gently took the journal from my hands. His fingers were steady, but his violet eyes were wet, and when he spoke, his voice was a low, rough scrape of sound.

"Theron killed himself," he said.

The words hit me like a physical blow. I stared at him, my mouth opening, but no sound came out.

"Not immediately. He served for another twelve years. He was beloved by the palace staff. He smiled at the servants. He trained his successor with patience and kindness. And then, on the morning of his sixty-eighth birthday, he walked into the royal gardens, sat down beneath his favorite jasmine trellis, and quietly, gently unraveled his own soul from his body. He left a note. It said: 'I can no longer bear to be perceived.'"

My father closed his eyes. "Godric restored him, of course. Instantly. Effortlessly. Theron woke up on the garden bench with his soul perfectly intact and a warm cup of jasmine tea placed beside him. Godric had left a small, handwritten note: 'You are seen, Theron, and you are loved. Please stay.' Theron served for another twenty-three years after that. He died of natural causes at ninety-one, surrounded by his grandchildren. He never spoke of the Null-Silence again."

The cognitive dissonance was a physical blade twisting in my gut. The same God who had smelled a void-entity into screaming, melting physicality had also written a kind, tender note to a suicidal wizard and restored his soul without a second thought. The warmth and the horror were two sides of the same infinite, incomprehensible coin, and my mortal mind was fracturing against the weight of holding both truths simultaneously.

"Three more," my father said, and his voice was not unkind. "Keep reading, Kaedan."

---

**THE STAT JOURNAL OF MAREK, 67TH COURT WIZARD OF THRONEGATE**
**YEAR 399,721 OF THE ETERNAL REIGN — THE INCIDENT OF LUCK**

The handwriting was a chaotic, sprawling scrawl that seemed to laugh at itself even as it trembled across the page. The ink was a garish, almost offensive shade of bright crimson, and the margins were filled with small, hysterical doodles of dice, playing cards, and tiny, screaming stick figures.

*I am Marek. I am the 67th Court Wizard. I am writing this journal because I must, because it is tradition, because my father would rise from his grave and strangle me if I did not, but I must confess something before I begin: I am laughing. I have been laughing for approximately six hours. The palace healers have checked my brain for damage and found nothing. My wife has slapped me twice. My son has asked if I am drunk. I am not drunk. I am simply unable to stop laughing at the sheer, exquisite, cosmic absurdity of what I witnessed. The Determinant Order is dead. They died in the most statistically improbable cascade of self-destruction in the history of existence. And Godric did absolutely nothing to cause it. Do you understand? Nothing. He sat on His throne. He drank His tea. He asked me about the weather. And the most powerful, certainty-based civilization in the omniverse destroyed itself through a sequence of events so wildly, impossibly unlikely that the probability of it happening was one divided by infinity divided by infinity. I am laughing because it is the only alternative to screaming, and my throat is already raw from screaming.*

*The Determinant Order was not a military power. They were a hyper-advanced civilization of probability engineers who had achieved what they called "Absolute Certainty." They had spent five million years systematically eliminating randomness from their domain. Every electron orbit was calculated. Every quantum fluctuation was predicted and compensated for. Every decision, from the trajectory of a raindrop to the outcome of a galactic war, was known with exactly one hundred percent certainty before it occurred. They had never, in the entire history of their civilization, experienced a single unexpected event. They had conquered nine hundred decillion multiverses not through force, but through the simple, undeniable fact that their victory was always, with absolute certainty, the only possible outcome.*

*They were the masters of fate. They had made luck obsolete.*

*And they decided that Godric was a variable that needed to be eliminated from the equation of existence.*

*Their plan was, by the standards of mortal logic, flawless. They spent seven hundred thousand years calculating every possible variable. They accounted for His Strength, His Speed, His Senses, His Magic, His Intelligence, His Charisma, His Wisdom, His Durability, and every other measurable and immeasurable attribute in His existence. They built a probability engine the size of a supermassive galaxy cluster, powered by the conceptual certainty of their entire civilization. The engine was designed to calculate the exact, singular outcome where Godric's infinite defenses all simultaneously failed. The probability of this outcome was, by their own calculations, exactly zero percent. But the engine was designed to force zero-probability events into reality through the sheer, overwhelming weight of accumulated certainty.*

*They activated the engine. And Godric, possessing absolute omniscience, knew the exact instant they began their seven-hundred-thousand-year preparation. He knew every equation they derived, every engine component they forged, every variable they calculated. He allowed them to exist for seven hundred thousand years because He was curious. He wanted to see what would happen if He simply did not intervene. He wanted to test His Luck.*

*On the day of the attack, Godric sat upon His throne. He was holding a cup of Earl Grey tea. He looked at me, His warm, fatherly eyes crinkling at the corners, and said: "Marek, do you believe in luck?"*

*I did not know how to answer. Before I could try, the Determinant Order's probability engine activated across the dimensional barrier. It was the most sophisticated machine ever built in the history of creation. It had calculated the outcome with absolute, one hundred percent certainty.*

*And then Godric's Luck, which had been multiplying infinitely every Planck second for nearly four hundred thousand years, simply existed in the same conceptual space as the probability engine.*

*The engine calculated the outcome. It factored in every variable, including—for the first time—the Luck stat of the being it was targeting. The engine's certainty matrix immediately, catastrophically inverted. The probability value of Godric's Luck was not infinite. It was beyond infinite. It was a value that made the concept of probability itself meaningless. The engine, faced with a variable that shattered the foundational logic of its existence, did the only thing a certainty-based machine could do: it recalculated. And it recalculated. And it recalculated. It found that the most certain, most probable, most statistically guaranteed outcome of attacking Godric was complete, immediate, and agonizing self-destruction.*

*The engine issued a single, galaxy-wide broadcast: "Error. Victory probability: negative infinity. Recommended action: cease to exist." And then the engine self-destructed.*

*The cascade was instantaneous, and it was beautiful in the most horrifying way imaginable. The probability engine's self-destruction sent a feedback pulse through the certainty-grid that connected every single member of the Determinant Order. The pulse did not kill them. It simply reversed their certainty. Every electron that had been perfectly calculated suddenly jumped to a random orbit. Every quantum fluctuation that had been suppressed suddenly expressed itself with violent, unpredictable force. The High Certainty Council, assembled to watch their inevitable victory, spontaneously combusted. Not all of them. Only the ones who had eaten a specific, statistically improbable breakfast. The Chief Probability Engineer choked to death on a sip of water—an event so unlikely that the probability of it occurring naturally was equivalent to a single atom randomly teleporting across the entire diameter of the omniverse. The Grand Determinant, the immortal, all-knowing leader of the Order, tripped on a perfectly smooth floor, fell down a flight of stairs that had never existed before, and broke every single bone in his conceptual body simultaneously.*

*It was absurd. It was ridiculous. It was the entire civilization of nine hundred decillion multiverses being systematically, individually destroyed by a cascading chain of one-in-infinity events that all spontaneously resolved to one-hundred-percent probability at the exact same instant. Ships collided with debris that had no physical source. Weapons misfired and struck their wielders in statistically impossible ricochets. The Order's certainty-grid, the foundation of their entire existence, spontaneously recalculated that the most certain path to absolute victory was to dissolve every single member into a fine, bloody mist of confused, screaming atoms.*

*The collateral damage was exactly zero. Not a single innocent life was lost. Not a single neutral multiverse was harmed. Every absurd, impossible death was perfectly, surgically precise. Luck, it turns out, is the most precise weapon in existence when it belongs to Godric.*

*When it was over, Godric lowered His teacup. He looked at the empty space where the Determinant Order had once existed, and He nodded slowly. "Fascinating," He said. "I did not even have to try."*

*He looked at me, His warm smile returning. "Marek, would you like more tea?"*

*I said yes. I am still laughing. I think I will be laughing for the rest of my life. The joke is simply too good. The masters of certainty, the eliminators of luck, the civilization that had never experienced a single unexpected event, were destroyed by a God whose Luck they had failed to factor into their equation. They spent seven hundred thousand years calculating every variable. They missed one. And that one variable was, quite literally, infinitely larger than the sum of all other variables in existence.*

*I am going to stop writing now. My wife is calling me for dinner. I will serve my King until I die. I will love Him. I will laugh at His cosmic jokes. And I will never, ever play dice with Him.*

---

A short, choked sound escaped my throat. It was almost a laugh, but it was strangled by the weight of everything else I had read. My father's expression did not change, but I saw the faintest twitch at the corner of his mouth—a ghost of the same dark, hysterical amusement that had consumed Marek.

"Marek died laughing," my father said. "Literally. At the age of eighty-four, on his deathbed, surrounded by his children and grandchildren. He was telling them the story of the Determinant Order—the sanitized version, the one that didn't include the melting flesh and the screaming crystal spheres—and he laughed so hard that his heart finally gave out. The healers said it was the happiest death they had ever witnessed."

I stared at the crimson ink and the scribbled stick figures. "That is... that is..."

"Absurd," my father finished. "Yes. That is the point, Kaedan. His Luck is not just power. It is the fundamental, inescapable truth that any opposition to Godric will fail in the most statistically improbable, humiliating, and absolute way possible. There is no version of reality where resistance succeeds. The probability is not zero. It is less than zero. It is a negative infinity that actively makes the opposition destroy itself."

He reached across the table and took my hand. His palm was warm, calloused, and steady. "One more journal, Kaedan. Mine."

---

**THE STAT JOURNAL OF CORVIN, 112TH COURT WIZARD OF THRONEGATE**
**YEAR 999,970 OF THE ETERNAL REIGN — THE INCIDENT OF CHARISMA**

The handwriting was achingly familiar. I had grown up watching those same careful, deliberate letters form on parchment as my father wrote letters to the palace staff, notes to my mother, and the countless administrative documents that came with the role of Court Wizard. But this handwriting was different. It was heavier. The pen had pressed harder into the parchment, leaving furrows in the fibers. The ink was a deep, dark violet—the color of our bloodline's amplified mana—and the pages smelled faintly of pipe tobacco and the sharp, clean scent of the mountain wind that blew through my father's private study.

*My name is Corvin. I am one hundred and twelfth in the line of Blake. I am fifty-eight years old, and I have served my King for thirty-eight years. I have witnessed a thousand miracles and a thousand horrors. I have seen Him restore entire civilizations with a passing thought. I have seen Him erase entire concepts with less effort than it takes to draw a breath. I have loved Him with the fierce, unshakeable devotion of a loyal subject. I have feared Him with the primal, bone-deep terror of a mortal standing in the shadow of an infinite, recursive godhead. But nothing—nothing I had ever experienced or read in the hundred and forty-six journals before this one—prepared me for what happened when the Throne of Wills came to Thronegate.*

*The Throne of Wills were not warriors. They were not sorcerers. They were not probability engineers or temporal predators or crystalline titans. They were the most charismatic beings in the omniverse. Their entire civilization was built upon the power of presence, the ability to command absolute loyalty through sheer, overwhelming force of personality. They had conquered five hundred decillion multiverses—not through violence, but through the simple, undeniable fact that anyone who met them, anyone who heard them speak, anyone who simply stood in their presence, would inevitably, irrevocably fall into a state of complete, worshipful subjugation. They did not break wills. They replaced wills with an all-consuming, ecstatic devotion to their own magnificence.*

*They arrived at the capital without weapons, without armies, without a single hostile intention—or so they claimed. Their High Sovereign, a being of such radiant, heartbreaking beauty that merely looking at her caused the palace guards to weep tears of involuntary adoration, strode into the grand reception hall with the confidence of a being who had never, in the entire history of her existence, encountered a will stronger than her own.*

*I was standing at Godric's side. I felt her charisma wash over me like a warm, golden wave, and I felt my own will begin to fray at the edges. My thousandfold daily amplification, my vast arcane power, my decades of mental discipline—all of it began to dissolve under the sheer, crushing weight of her presence. I wanted to kneel. I wanted to weep. I wanted to offer her my soul and thank her for the privilege of doing so.*

*And then I looked at Godric. He was seated upon the crystalled throne, His ruggedly handsome face calm and unbothered, His heavily muscled frame relaxed against the velvet backing. He was looking at the High Sovereign with an expression of mild, almost bored curiosity. Her charisma—the most powerful force of personality in the omniverse—was washing over Him like a gentle spring breeze against a mountain of immovable granite.*

*The High Sovereign began her speech. It was a masterpiece of persuasive artistry. She spoke of the tyranny of Thronegate, the suffering of the subjugated multiverses, the need for a new order built upon mutual respect and shared governance. Every word was perfectly chosen. Every intonation was flawlessly delivered. Every rhetorical flourish was devastatingly effective. I felt myself swaying, nodding along, my mind filling with the warm, golden certainty that she was right, that she was just, that she was the savior the omniverse had been waiting for.*

*Godric listened. He listened to the entire speech without interrupting. And when the High Sovereign finished, her radiant face glowing with the absolute, unshakeable confidence of a being who had never failed to sway a single audience in her entire existence, Godric did something that I will never, ever forget.*

*He raised one eyebrow.*

*That was it. A single, microscopic muscular contraction that lifted His left eyebrow by approximately two micrometers. The expression lasted for an infinitesimal fraction of a Planck second. It was not a threat. It was not a display of power. It was the facial equivalent of a man politely, wordlessly expressing mild skepticism at a particularly bold claim made during casual dinner conversation.*

*But the expression was unshielded. Godric's absolute concealment, which normally suppressed the crushing weight of His presence, slipped by an amount so infinitesimally small that calling it zero percent would be an infinitely generous overestimation. And through that microscopic gap, the High Sovereign perceived—for the first time in her existence—the true Charisma of the being she had dared to lecture.*

*Godric's Charisma, like all His attributes, had been multiplying infinitely by itself every single Planck second for nearly a million years. It was not a force of persuasion. It was not a charm or a glamour or a magical compulsion. It was the fundamental, absolute, undeniable truth of His existence—the raw, unfiltered majesty of a being who was, in every measurable and immeasurable sense, infinitely superior to everything else in creation. The High Sovereign, whose entire identity was built upon being the most charismatic entity in the omniverse, was suddenly, violently confronted with the reality that she was nothing. Less than nothing. A single candle flame trying to outshine a billion supernovas.*

*She did not surrender. She did not kneel. She did not switch her allegiance and offer her services to the King. That would have been the logical, merciful outcome. But Godric is not merciful to those who oppose Him. His Charisma, expressed through that microscopic eyebrow raise, did not persuade the High Sovereign. It annihilated her.*

*She saw, for one single, eternal Planck instant, the true scope of her own inadequacy. She saw the infinite, recursive majesty of the being she had tried to sway. She saw the absolute, unbridgeable chasm between her finite charisma and His infinite, multiplying, beyond-mathematical presence. And the cognitive dissonance—the violent, irreconcilable contradiction between her lifelong certainty of her own supremacy and the undeniable reality of His absolute godhood—physically tore her mind apart.*

*She screamed. It was not a scream of fear or pain. It was a scream of pure, unadulterated self-loathing—the sound of a being whose entire identity had just been proven fundamentally, irreparably false. She clawed at her own radiant face with her perfect, manicured nails, gouging deep, bloody furrows into her cheeks and forehead. She tore out her own eyes, weeping and laughing and screaming simultaneously, offering the wet, glistening orbs to Godric like a supplicant presenting a sacrifice to an angry deity. She ripped open her own chest with her bare hands, cracking her sternum with the sickening, wet crunch of shattering bone, and pulled out her still-beating heart, holding it aloft in trembling, blood-slicked palms as she collapsed to her knees on the marble floor.*

*Her entourage—two hundred of the most charismatic beings in the omniverse—followed her into self-destruction. They clawed, bit, tore, and mutilated themselves in an orgy of ecstatic, worshipful self-annihilation. They gouged out eyes, ripped out tongues, shattered bones, and spilled their own blood across the immaculate marble in a collective act of absolute, horrified devotion. The High Sovereign died with her heart in her hands and a smile on her ruined, bleeding face—a smile of pure, unadulterated relief that she had finally been permitted to witness the true majesty of existence before she ceased to be part of it.*

*The throne room fell silent. The marble floor was slick with blood and viscera. The air smelled of copper and regret and the faint, lingering ozone of shattered charisma. Godric lowered His eyebrow. He looked at the pile of self-destroyed corpses, and His expression was one of mild, almost bored disinterest.*

*"They talked too much," He said.*

*He looked at me. His warm, fatherly smile returned, crinkling the corners of His eyes. "Corvin, would you mind having someone clean this up? The blood will stain the marble if it sits too long."*

*I said yes, of course I said yes. I have never said no to Him. I will never say no to Him. I love Him with every fiber of my being, and I fear Him with every screaming, fractured corner of my soul, and I will serve Him faithfully until the day I die because I have seen what happens to those who oppose Him, and I have no desire to tear out my own heart and offer it as a sacrifice to His infinite, indifferent majesty.*
